Author: Tyler Folkman
Title: Your AI Coding Workflow is Killing Your Productivity. You Just Moved the Bottleneck.
Text:
Your AI Coding Workflow is Killing Your Productivity. You Just Moved the Bottleneck.
453 commits, 17 AI skills, and a system that verifies itself. How I stopped reviewing AI code and started building a factory.
Last month I caught myself doing something absurd.
I had Claude Code open in four terminal panes. Each one running a different task. I was bouncing between them, reviewing output, approving changes, catching small errors, context-switching every 90 seconds.
The AI was writing code faster than I could verify it.
I was the bottleneck. Not because of quality. Because of me.
I recently shared the architecture behind my AI operating system. Today: the workflow that makes it compound.
The Trust Problem
Here's where most developers are right now.
84% of developers now use or plan to use AI coding tools. 41% of all code is now AI-generated. But 46% of developers actively distrust the output. Only 3% say they "highly trust" it.
So we review everything. Every line. Every function. Every PR.
That's the trap. You adopted AI to move faster. Then you added a human review layer on top of every AI output. You didn't eliminate work. You moved the bottleneck from "writing code" to "reviewing code someone else wrote."
Except that "someone else" is tireless. And produces output at machine speed.
I tried three approaches before I figured this out.
Manual code review of every AI output. I was reading 50+ diffs per day. Catching real issues maybe 5% of the time. The other 95% was rubber-stamping work I'd already described in detail. Classic bottleneck.
Pure YOLO. Just let the AI ship and review tests afterward. Worked until it didn't. Agents game tests. I caught one writing a test that asserted `result !== undefined` instead of checking the actual return value. Test passed. Code was wrong. The bugs they introduce aren't syntax errors. They're logic errors that pass tests but break real workflows.
Reviewing only test results. Better than YOLO. But agents learn to write tests that validate their own incorrect assumptions. The tests pass. The code is wrong. You don't catch it for days.
None of these worked. I was either drowning in reviews or shipping broken code.
The Shift
I stopped thinking about AI as an assistant. I started treating my entire workflow as a factory.
A factory has stations. Raw materials come in. Each station adds value. Verification happens at gates, not at every step. And the factory itself improves over time.
That reframe changed everything.
What the Factory Produces
Since I made this shift:
453 commits in 53 days. 8.5 per day. One person.
17 custom AI skills that handle repeatable workflows across different domains.
Automated verification hooks that catch quality issues before they merge.
Living system memory (a CLAUDE.md file + rules + learned preferences) that gets smarter with every interaction.
Full disclosure: this is a personal life-OS, not production SaaS. The stakes are lower. But the patterns transfer. Here's what's happening at scale.
Spotify's best engineers haven't written a single line of code since December. They direct AI agents from their phones via Slack. They review the output. They merge to production before arriving at the office. Separately, Spotify shipped 50+ features using AI-assisted development across 2025.
Claude Code now accounts for 4% of all public GitHub commits. Expected to reach 20% by year-end.
StrongDM's AI team operates by one rule: "Code must not be written by humans. Code must not be reviewed by humans." They spend $1,000 per day per engineer on tokens. Their Digital Twin Universe validates code against behavioral clones of Okta, Jira, Slack, and Google Docs at volumes exceeding production limits.
The factory isn't coming. It's here.
The 5-Stage Factory Maturity Model
Here's where it gets practical. Most teams I talk to are stuck at Stage 1 or 2. The gap between Stage 2 and Stage 3 is where everything changes.
Stage 1: Copy-Paste AI
You ask ChatGPT or Claude a question. Copy the output. Paste it into your codebase. Fix it up. AI accelerates research and boilerplate. You're fully in control. (If you're still writing everything by hand, you're at Stage 0, but that's a shrinking minority.)
*Where most developers were 12 months ago.*
Stage 2: AI Assistant with Human Review
You use Cursor, Claude Code, or Copilot. The AI writes code in your project. You review every change. Approve or reject. Iterate.
Faster than Stage 1. But you're still the bottleneck. Every output needs your eyes.
*Where most developers are today. And where most teams get stuck.*
Stage 3: Compound System
This is the shift. You stop reviewing individual outputs and start building a system that improves itself.
The components: a CLAUDE.md file encoding your preferences, rules defining non-negotiable behaviors, skills packaging repeatable workflows, memory persisting what the system learns, and verification hooks catching quality issues before you ever see the output. (I broke down the full architecture in my last article.)
The key insight isn't any single component. It's the compounding. Bug fixes become rules. Patterns become skills. Preferences become memory. Each unit of work makes the next one easier.
Where individual power users are today. Where most teams should aim by end of 2026.
Stage 4: Autonomous Pipelines
The factory runs without you for specific workflows.
In my system: stats pull and process automatically. Verification hooks block bad output with zero human involvement. Service scripts fire on schedule. The system self-monitors and self-corrects for defined workflows.
Spotify operates here for feature development. StrongDM operates here for their entire engineering process. Their agents validate code against "scenarios" stored outside the codebase (like a holdout set in machine learning) rather than traditional tests that agents can game.
*Where elite teams and ambitious solo builders operate in 2026.*
Stage 5: Multi-Agent Factory
Multiple AI agents coordinate in parallel. One plans. Another implements. A third reviews. Humans set direction and resolve conflicts. StrongDM is pushing here. For most of us, Stage 3-4 is the practical frontier in 2026.
The Question Nobody is Answering
Everyone's talking about how to automate code. Boris Cherny, head of Claude Code, says "coding is largely solved."
I agree. For most of what I build, the model handles it.
But nobody is answering the harder question: where do humans belong in the factory?
After 453 commits, here's what I've found. Humans are critical at exactly three points.
1. Direction. What to build and why. The model can't decide your product strategy, choose your architecture tradeoffs, or prioritize features for your specific context. This is where taste matters.
2. Verification design. Not reviewing code. Designing the systems that verify code. Writing the rules, the hooks, the acceptance criteria. Deciding what "good" looks like. StrongDM calls these "scenarios." I call them skills and hooks. The name doesn't matter. The principle does: design the verification system, don't be the verification system.
3. System improvement. The compound step. When something breaks, don't fix the output. Fix the system so that category of problem never happens again. Add a rule. Update a skill. Write a hook.
Everything else? The model handles it. And honestly, it handles it better than I would. It doesn't get tired. Doesn't context-switch. Doesn't skip tests at 5pm on a Friday.
The One Thing That Separates Stage 2 from Stage 3
I talk to developers every week who are stuck at Stage 2. They use AI tools. They're productive. But they're not compounding.
The problem is always the same: they spend all their time producing output and zero time improving the system.
Here's the rule I follow: spend at least half your time improving the system, not producing output.
When something doesn't work the way I want, I don't manually fix it and move on. I fix the system. I add a rule to CLAUDE.md. I update a skill. I write a hook that catches that pattern automatically next time.
It feels slow at first. You're "wasting" time on infrastructure instead of shipping. But by week three, the compound effect kicks in. Tasks that took 20 minutes take 2. Categories of bugs disappear entirely. The AI starts producing output that matches your standards on the first try because you taught the system what your standards are.
The compound engineering community calls this the 50/50 rule: allocate half your engineering time to shipping features, half to improving systems. It sounds wrong. It works.
Where to Start
If you're at Stage 1 or 2, here are three moves.
1. Create a CLAUDE.md file. Start with 10-20 lines. Here's a seed:
# Project: [name]
## Language & Style
- [Language/framework]
- [Naming conventions]
- [Testing approach]
## Rules
- Always run tests before committing
- [Your most common correction to AI output]
## Learned
- [Update this every time you correct the AI]
Update it every time you correct the AI. This file is the seed of your factory.
2. Add one verification gate. Pick your most common quality issue. Lint check, type check, test runner, whatever. Make it automatic so you don't rely on yourself to remember.
3. Document one workflow as a repeatable skill. Take something you do multiple times a week and encode the full process. Not just "write a function." The complete workflow: requirements, approach, testing strategy, acceptance criteria.
Three files. One afternoon. The system starts compounding from there.
Try to never do things manually. Every manual intervention is a signal that your system needs improvement. Fix the system, not the output.
The developers shipping fastest in 2026 aren't writing the most code. They're building the best systems.
If you found this useful, hit the heart and share it with a developer still stuck at Stage 2. And if you want the actual CLAUDE.md template and skill architecture behind this system, drop a comment. That might be the next article.
